description: On a woody stem or root, 'koremu' is the outer protective tissue, most familiar as the visible covering of a tree. Its thickness, texture, and living layers vary with species and age. 'wolea' (wood) is the structural material beneath.
articulatory_notes: The back of the tongue closes against the soft palate for 'k' and releases into rounded 'o'. A tap or brief trill begins stressed 're'; the lips then meet for nasal 'm' and open onto high-back rounded 'u'.
usage_notes: A plant or tree precedes the noun: 'shiro koremu' is tree bark and 'pilomu koremu' sycamore bark. Once removed and treated as material, ordinary object classification can replace the living classifier.

semantic_domains.nature: protective outer tissue on woody stems and roots
semantic_domains.physical: a textured outer layer that can also be handled as material
